text: All living cells require nutrients, oxygen, and removal of waste substances for proper functioning.
Efficient transport systems are required to move substances to and from cells in multicellular organisms.
Simple organisms like sponges and coelenterates circulate water through body cavities for exchange of substances.
Complex organisms use specialized fluids like blood to transport substances within the body.
Blood is a connective tissue consisting of plasma and formed elements.
Plasma is a straw-coloured viscous fluid that constitutes about 55 percent of blood.
Plasma contains 90–92 percent water and 6–8 percent proteins.
Major plasma proteins include fibrinogen, globulins, and albumins.
Fibrinogen is responsible for blood clotting.
Globulins are involved in immune defense mechanisms.
Albumins help maintain osmotic balance in blood.
Plasma contains minerals, glucose, amino acids, and lipids in dissolved form.
Serum is plasma without clotting factors.
Formed elements include erythrocytes, leucocytes, and platelets.
Red blood cells are the most abundant cells in blood.
Red blood cells transport respiratory gases using haemoglobin.
Mammalian red blood cells are biconcave and lack a nucleus.
Red blood cells have an average lifespan of 120 days.
Red blood cells are destroyed in the spleen.
White blood cells are nucleated and lack haemoglobin.
White blood cells are involved in immune defense.
White blood cells are classified into granulocytes and agranulocytes.
Platelets are cell fragments involved in blood clotting.
Platelets are produced from megakaryocytes in bone marrow.
ABO blood grouping is based on the presence of A and B antigens on red blood cells.
Blood transfusion requires compatibility between donor and recipient to prevent clotting.
Blood group O is called universal donor because it lacks A and B antigens.
Blood group AB is called universal recipient because it has no antibodies against A or B antigens.
Rh factor is an antigen present on red blood cells in most humans.
Rh-negative individuals can develop antibodies if exposed to Rh-positive blood.
Erythroblastosis foetalis occurs when Rh antibodies from mother destroy fetal red blood cells.
Blood clotting prevents excessive blood loss after injury.
Fibrin forms a network that traps blood cells to form a clot.
Blood clotting involves conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin through enzyme-mediated reactions.
Lymph is a colorless fluid derived from tissue fluid and circulates through lymphatic vessels.
Lymph transports nutrients, hormones, and immune cells.
Fats are absorbed into lymph through lacteals in intestinal villi.
Open circulatory system allows blood to flow into body cavities.
Closed circulatory system confines blood within vessels for regulated flow.
The human heart is a muscular organ with four chambers.
The heart pumps blood throughout the body via circulatory pathways.
Heart valves ensure one-way flow of blood and prevent backflow.
Sinoatrial node acts as the pacemaker of the heart.
Sinoatrial node generates electrical impulses that regulate heart rhythm.
Cardiac cycle consists of systole and diastole of heart chambers.
Stroke volume is the amount of blood pumped by a ventricle per beat.
Cardiac output is the volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute.
ECG records the electrical activity of the heart.
Double circulation involves separate pulmonary and systemic pathways.
Heart activity is regulated by autonomic nervous system and hormones.
Hypertension is a condition of persistently high blood pressure.
Coronary artery disease is caused by narrowing of arteries due to plaque deposition.
Heart failure occurs when the heart cannot pump sufficient blood to meet body needs.
The left ventricle has a thicker wall because it pumps blood to the entire body under high pressure.
Heart valves ensure unidirectional flow to prevent backflow of blood.
Double circulation prevents mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, increasing efficiency.
Biconcave shape of RBCs increases surface area for efficient gas exchange.
Arteries carry blood away from the heart under high pressure, while veins return blood to the heart under low pressure.
Open circulation allows blood to flow into body cavities, while closed circulation keeps blood within vessels.
RBCs transport gases using haemoglobin, while WBCs are involved in immune defense.
If heart valves fail, blood may flow backward, reducing pumping efficiency.
If the sinoatrial node fails, heart rhythm becomes irregular and requires artificial pacemaker support.
Blockage in arteries reduces blood supply to tissues and may lead to tissue damage or heart attack.
Low platelet count leads to excessive bleeding due to impaired clotting.
Low hemoglobin reduces oxygen transport capacity, leading to fatigue and weakness.
Tricuspid valve is located between right atrium and right ventricle.
Mitral valve is located between left atrium and left ventricle.
Atrial systole pushes blood from atria into ventricles.
Ventricular systole pumps blood into pulmonary artery and aorta.
P wave represents atrial depolarization.
QRS complex represents ventricular depolarization.
T wave represents ventricular repolarization.
Fishes have a 2-chambered heart with one atrium and one ventricle. The heart pumps deoxygenated blood to the gills for oxygenation and then to the body (single circulation).
Amphibians have a 3-chambered heart with two atria and one ventricle. Oxygenated and deoxygenated blood mix in the ventricle, leading to incomplete double circulation.
Reptiles (except crocodiles) have a 3-chambered heart with an incomplete septum, causing partial mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood (incomplete double circulation).
Crocodiles, birds, and mammals have a 4-chambered heart with two atria and two ventricles. Oxygenated and deoxygenated blood are completely separated, resulting in efficient double circulation.
